dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, respond to a priority 1 breathing problem at --address redacted--, Apartment 611.
Hello sir, I'm Josh. I'm an EMT with the wellness crew. What made you call 911?
Shortness of breath, been like this for over an hour now.
Mr. Charlie, can you tell me your name for me?
Charles, but most call me Charlie.
Mr. Charlie, do you know where you are?
I'm at my home in apartment #611.
Very good. What is today's date?
The 21st of December.
That's correct. What changed today to make you call 911?
I could not breathe properly and got scared.
Mr. Charlie, can you tell me more about your breathing trouble?
I took albuterol earlier, but it didn't work. Breathing is still difficult.
You mentioned COPD, can you tell me more about it?
Yes, I've had it a long time. I try to manage with inhalers, but today, they didn't help.
Can I get your age and weight?
I'm 68 and about 220 pounds.
Mr. Charlie, do you have any other health concerns?
I have high blood pressure, heart issues, and diabetes not requiring insulin.
Do you smoke or use tobacco regularly?
I smoke about half a pack a day.
Any chest pain, dizziness, nausea, or vomiting today?
No chest pain, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, or lightheadedness. Just shortness of breath.
Have you been around anyone who's sick or taken a test for Covid-19 recently?
No, I haven't been around anyone who's ill and I haven't had a test.
Mr. Charlie, your airway is open and shows no signs of blockage.
Mr. Charlie, your breathing was initially faster, but has slowed to around 12.
We heard some rhonchi in the lower lobes of your lungs.
Your pulse remains strong and regular.
Your pulse is 83 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 162 over 86.
Respirations have come down to 12 per minute.
Your oxygen saturation is 94%.
We attempted to check your glucose, but were not able to get a reading.
We did a quick stroke assessment and found no signs of stroke.
On a scale from 1 to 10, how bad is the breathing difficulty?
There's no pain, just difficulty breathing.
We're giving you 2 liters of oxygen through a nasal cannula. This might help you breathe better.
Mr. Charlie, does the oxygen seem to be helping?
Yes, I can breathe a bit easier now.
We're placing an ETCO2 monitor to get a more detailed view of your breathing.
It reads 30 with a normal waveform, which looks good.
We're now placing four EKG leads to monitor your heart's rhythm.
The first session shows a ventricular paced rhythm.
We'll now take a full 12-lead EKG for a more complete look.
The 12-lead shows a ventricular paced rhythm as well, nothing unusual otherwise.
Considering your COPD history and current improvement with oxygen, we're following protocol for respiratory distress.
Protocol 3-11 for medical respiratory distress fits your current condition.
Let's now reassess your vitals. Your pulse was previously 83.
Right now, your pulse is at 77, a slight decrease.
Breathing rate is also down to around 12 per minute.
Your blood pressure is now 151 over 98, and oxygen level up to 95%.
The oxygen treatment is helping your breathing, Mr. Charlie.
We've done another round of 4-lead EKG monitoring and still see a ventricular paced rhythm.
Yes, I feel a little better than earlier.
To confirm, you said you have not been exposed to anyone with a virus or tested recently?
Yes, that's accurate. I've been alone and haven't had any exposure.
We're going to St. Mary's Hospital to get you evaluated further.
Since your breathing has improved and your vitals are stable, this will be a non-emergent transport.
Fair enough. I'm fine with that.
We'll now safely secure you to the stretcher.
Since you could take 5 steps, it'll be an ambulatory transport to the stretcher.
He's a 68-year-old with COPD who had a non-emergent exacerbation that improved with oxygen.
He initially refused transport but changed his mind when breathing worsened. All vitals are stable.
We've handed him over to an RN at St. Mary's with a full report, including EKG and medical history.
Used the last nasal cannula for ETCO2 monitoring from the unit. Dispatch has been notified.
Mr. Charlie, can you tell me if you've been feeling dizzy at all today?
No, I haven't been dizzy or lightheaded.
Your skin is warm, dry, and normal in color.
Your ETCO2 remains at 30, and we don't see any changes in waveform.
We are displaying a final 4-lead rhythm strip that still shows the ventricularpaced rhythm.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate your breathing difficulty now?
There's still no pain-you're just struggling with breathing now.
Secured to stretcher via 5 point harness for transport.
Vitals now include a pulse of 77, respirations of 12, and blood pressure of 151/98.
Also, O2 sat stable at 95%, no changes in pattern over last hour.
Oxygen administered at 2 liters via nasal cannula for the entire transport.
No signs of stroke, no signs of infection, and vitals remained stable throughout.
Mr. Charlie denies any new symptoms since initial contact.
No medications administered on scene except oxygen for respiratory distress.
No history of allergies and no significant reaction to any prior treatments on record.
Preserved mental status throughout, no change in responsiveness.
Final verbal report given to staff upon arrival at St. Mary's at location bed 6.
All items including used ETCO2 cannula were accounted for and communicated to dispatch.
Now, official handover to hospital medical team proceeding smoothly.
St. Mary's received confirming non-emergent transport protocol.
Records and documentation handed to hospital nurse preparing for reception.
Patient proceeded outpatient under ambulatory non-ambulance transport protocol as assessed.
Ambulatory transport utilized due to patient cooperation and stable mental state.
Are there any other symptoms you've had throughout the past 24 hours?
No new symptoms, just shortness of breath initially and now stable, with no other concerns.
We performed several EKG monitoring readings and your rhythm is consistent.
EKG report showed consistent results, still paced rhythm with nothing alarming.
Your airway is still patent and no changes have been noted in the interval.
Are there any new illnesses or symptoms that you haven't reported?
No new ailments have developed and my condition remains the same.
Your respiratory pattern remains stable with no signs of labored breathing.
Oxygen levels remain at 95%, which is a good result after initial tidal increase you had with oxygen support.
Once we reach the hospital you will be non-ambulatory as per case phase status noted.
Transport planned correctly, both patient and crew safe and no incident during transit.
